title: Act I
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: ANDREI PROZOROV. NATASHA, his fiancée, afterward his wife. OLGA, MASHA, IRINA, his sisters. VERSHININ, Lieutenant-Colonel, battery commander. TUZENBACH, Baron, Lieutenant. SOLYONY, Captain. CHEBUTYKIN, Army Doctor. FEDOTIK, Second Lieutenant. RODE, Second Lieutenant. FERAPONT, old porter at the county council. ANFISA, old nurse.
ACT I

The house of the PROZOROV family. A drawing-room with columns. Behind the drawing-room, a large ballroom is visible. Midday. Outside it is sunny and cheerful. The table in the ballroom is being laid for lunch.

OLGA, in the dark blue uniform dress of a schoolmistress, is correcting exercise books all day long. MASHA, in a black dress, her hat on her knees, is sitting reading a book. IRINA, in white, stands lost in thought.

OLGA: Father died exactly a year ago today—the fifth of May, your name-day, Irina. It was very cold and snowing then. I thought I should not survive it, and you were lying in a dead faint. And now a year has gone by and we can think of it calmly; you are wearing white again and your face is radiant—(The clock strikes twelve.) The clock struck twelve then too. (A pause.) I remember that a band played as they carried Father, and they fired a salute at the cemetery. He was a general, in command of a brigade, yet there were few people there. Of course, it was raining then, raining hard, and snowing.
IRINA: Why think about it?
(BARON TUZENBACH, CHEBUTYKIN, and SOLYONY appear by the table in the ballroom.)

OLGA: It is warm today, we can have the windows open, and still the birches are not in bud. Father had his brigade for eleven years and left this town with us. I remember perfectly that in Moscow in early May everything is already in flower, it's warm, everything is flooded with sunshine. Eleven years ago, and yet I remember it all as if we had left yesterday. Oh, God! I woke up this morning and saw a flood of sunshine, saw the spring, and joy stirred in my heart. I had such a longing to be back home again!
CHEBUTYKIN: The devil it is!
TUZENBACH: Of course, it's all nonsense.
(MASHA, brooding over her book, softly whistles a tune.)

OLGA: Don't whistle, Masha. How can you! (A pause.) Being at school every day and then giving lessons till the evening gives me constant headaches, and the thoughts I have are those of an old woman already. And really these four years that I have been at the high school I have felt my youth and strength ebbing away drop by drop. And only one yearning grows stronger and stronger—
IRINA: To go to Moscow. To sell the house, to make an end of everything here, and off to Moscow.
OLGA: Yes! To Moscow as soon as possible.
(CHEBUTYKIN and TUZENBACH laugh.)
IRINA: Andrei will be a professor, he will be a professor anyway. But he won't live here. The only problem is poor Masha.
OLGA: Masha can come to Moscow for the whole summer, every year.
(MASHA is softly whistling a tune.)

IRINA: Please God it will work out. (Looking out of window.) How fine the weather is today. I don't know why I feel so lighthearted! I remembered this morning that it was my name-day and at once I felt joyful and thought of my childhood, when Mother was still alive. And such wonderful thoughts thrilled me, such thoughts!

OLGA: You are radiant today, you look lovely. Masha is lovely too. Andrei would be good-looking, but he has grown too fat and that does not suit him. And I've grown older and a great deal thinner. I suppose it's because I get so cross at the school. But today I am free, I am at home. I don't have a headache and I feel as though I am young again. I am only twenty-eight. All is well, all is from God, but it seems to me that if I were married and sitting at home all day, it would be better. (A pause.) I should love my husband.

TUZENBACH: (to SOLYONY) You talk such nonsense, I am tired of listening to you. (Coming into the drawing room.) I forgot to tell you, you will receive a visit today from Vershinin, the new battery commander. (Sits down at the piano.)
OLGA: Well, I shall be very glad to see him.
IRINA: Is he old?
TUZENBACH: No, not particularly. Forty, forty-five at the most. (Softly plays the piano.) He seems a nice fellow. Not stupid, that's certain. Only he talks a lot.
IRINA: Is he interesting?

TUZENBACH: He's all right, only he has a wife, a mother-in-law, and two little girls. And it's his second marriage too. He calls on everyone and tells them that he has a wife and two little girls. He'll tell you that too, I'm sure of it. His wife seems a little crazy, with her hair in a long braid like a girl's, and she always talks in a high-flown style, philosophizes, and frequently attempts to commit suicide, evidently to annoy her husband. I would have left a woman like that long ago, but he puts up with her and merely complains about her.

title: Act II
paragraphs: ACT II

The same scene as in Act I. Eight o'clock in the evening. In the street outside, only faintly audible, an accordion is being played. The stage is dark. NATASHA enters in a dressing-gown, carrying a candle.
NATASHA: (crossing the stage, opens a door, looks in) Andrei. She has come, she is eating in the dining-room, and they're eating pancakes. Andrei, it's a cold night. Put on your coat.
ANDREI: (appearing) Let them eat. I want to sit up and read a bit.
NATASHA: What? Sit up? But Andrei, it's gone eight. The children—
ANDREI: Natasha—
NATASHA: I'm not scolding, dear, but think of the children. You ought to go to bed at ten without fail, that's definite, you must. (She takes the candle and goes out.)
(VERSHININ and MASHA enter.)

VERSHININ: I have a headache from your samovar. (Laughs.) But I feel better now. Life is difficult. It seems dull and hopeless, and then some time passes, and you look around and life is full—so full!—and you wonder: why was I in despair? Life goes on and suddenly everything is quite clear. We Russians in particular love to torture ourselves with these questions, you know. When you are thirty you start wondering: where has my life gone? And then when you are forty, fifty, sixty—it seems the same. But life doesn't stop. It goes.

MASHA: (quietly) To know why one lives, or at least to know that one lives for something—that's important. But to live without knowing why you're alive—that's too hard. (Pause.) I feel a longing for life.

VERSHININ: Yes. (Pause.) In two hundred years—or three hundred—I don't know—life on earth will be unimaginably beautiful. People will not believe that there was ever a time like this, when we went to sleep unhappy, when we could not explain ourselves to each other. We are only preparing for them—working, suffering—creating, perhaps, something that they will know how to use. That is what we are here for.
MASHA: (softly) Tram-tam-tam.
VERSHININ: Tam-tam.
MASHA: Tra-ra-ra?
VERSHININ: Tra-ta-ta. (Laughs.)
(FEDOTIK comes in from outside.)
FEDOTIK: My luck is out completely! I went to the dance and then—nothing. I am cold and miserable.
(He goes out.)

VERSHININ: I've been thinking—what if I started life over again? What would I do differently? I think—I think I would not get married. No, no. (He laughs.) Everything would be exactly the same. I would make exactly the same mistakes, because that's what people are like.
MASHA: (smiling) You are the strangest man.
VERSHININ: Am I? (Pause.) I think about my daughters sometimes. I wonder what sort of women they will be. I wonder if life will be easier for them.
MASHA: I don't know. I think—I think happiness doesn't exist. Or if it does, it exists somewhere else, not here, not now. Always somewhere else.
(A long pause.)
VERSHININ: (quietly) I am very fond of you.
MASHA: (looking at him) I am fond of you too.

title: Act III
paragraphs: ACT III
The bedroom of OLGA and IRINA. A fire-alarm bell is ringing. There is a fire. Everyone is upset. It is three in the morning. OLGA and ANFISA are sorting linen. MASHA is sitting in a chair.

ANFISA: They are sitting downstairs under the stairs. I told them to come up. Why should people sit downstairs without their coats, I said, they'll freeze. (To MASHA.) Mashenka, you ought to have more Christian feeling—it's cold downstairs—
MASHA: (Getting up.) I'll go downstairs. (She goes out.)
OLGA: (sorting linen) Let them take them, Anfisa. We shan't want them. Let them take them.
ANFISA: Yes, yes, dear. Olga Sergeyevna, the whole of 1812 was not to compare—the French didn't hurt me as much—
OLGA: Go, nurse dear. Downstairs now.
ANFISA: (going) Yes, yes. I understand.
(IRINA enters with SOLYONY.)
OLGA: Irina, you look dreadful.
IRINA: Where is Masha?
OLGA: She went downstairs. You must rest. You look pale.
IRINA: (sitting down) I am tired. Today I've been working at the telegraph and I am so tired. The supervisor was unkind to me. He was rude. I cried.
OLGA: I know. I know.

IRINA: Oh, Olga, I cannot go on working at the telegraph, I cannot! I am so tired, so miserable. I am twenty-three already. I have been working for a long time, my brain has dried up. I've grown thin and old and ugly, and there's nothing—nothing. When I woke this morning and thought about my life and about all the things that were going to be done and were not done—
OLGA: Don't cry, child. I can't bear to see you cry.
IRINA: (controlling herself) There's no crying. I've done with crying. I used to cry when I was little. Every night I think: we shall be in Moscow. But a year goes by and we are still here—
OLGA: We will go. We will. Definitely, without fail—this autumn maybe. We will sell everything and go.
(The fire alarm outside grows louder, then fades.)
OLGA: But I am so tired. I have headaches every day. I feel like an old woman. And these four years in the school—I feel my youth leaving me, drop by drop. And one thought grows and grows—
IRINA: To Moscow.
OLGA: To Moscow.
(MASHA enters.)
MASHA: (sitting down) He loves me, he loves me not, he loves me, he loves me not—(laughing) So I am in love. I love him. I love Vershinin.
(Pause. OLGA and IRINA stare at her.)

MASHA: Well, what of it? I love him. It's fate. It means it's my lot. He loves me too. It's all dreadful. Is it dreadful? (Takes IRINA by the arm, draws her to her.) Oh, my darling. Somehow we shall live our life, and then, what will be will be. When you read a novel it all seems old and easy to understand, but when you fall in love yourself you see that nobody knows anything, and you have to decide everything for yourself.
OLGA: (putting her arms round both her sisters) I love you both—very much indeed. And now: to work. We must get through this night.

title: Act IV
paragraphs: ACT IV

The old garden of the Prozorov house. A long avenue of fir trees at the end of which is the river. On the other side of the river, a forest. On the right, the veranda of the house. The battery is moving, and they can be heard going by. It is autumn. Midday. CHEBUTYKIN sits alone in the garden.
IRINA: (going up to TUZENBACH) Now—we go to Moscow. Andrei has bought three tickets.
TUZENBACH: (smiling) There is something mysterious about the way you say Moscow. Moscow, Moscow—as though it were the promised land.
IRINA: We can't stay here, we simply can't. You can see—something must happen.
(A shot is heard in the distance. TUZENBACH pales.)
TUZENBACH: Something must happen. Yes. (Pause.) I love you, Irina. I love you so much—I've been very happy. I want you to know that.
IRINA: Nikolai, please don't—

TUZENBACH: I am not afraid of those pine-trees, those dark mysterious pine-trees, but I know that if you loved me I would be at home anywhere—even in the forest. (He pauses.) It's time I was off. There—do you see? There is a dead tree, but it still sways in the wind with the others. So I think that if I die, I shall still have part in life somehow. Good-bye, my darling. (He kisses her hands.) Your papers, the ones you gave me—are on my desk under the calendar.
IRINA: (softly) I'll come soon.
(TUZENBACH goes. MASHA, OLGA, and ANDREI appear from the house.)
(Another shot, closer.)
CHEBUTYKIN: (quietly, from his chair) He's been killed.
(MASHA stops.)
OLGA: What do you mean?
CHEBUTYKIN: Killed. (He looks at his newspaper.) In the duel. The baron is killed.
(A long silence. IRINA appears.)
IRINA: I know. I know. I knew he was going to die. I knew it. I felt it. I—(She is silent.)
(VERSHININ appears. He is in uniform with his baggage.)
VERSHININ: (to MASHA) I must say good-bye. My regiment is going. (He takes her hands.) Say good-bye for me. (MASHA weeps.) I must—say good-bye. Don't cry.
(He embraces MASHA, who cannot speak.)
VERSHININ: (softly) It has been so good, so good. All of it. Good-bye.
(He goes. MASHA is weeping.)
OLGA: (taking her in her arms) There, there, Masha dear.

MASHA: (weeping) The birds of passage are already flying away. (Looks up at the sky.) Swans or geese—oh, my happy, joyful life—what has become of it? Don't cry. Don't let us cry. (She wipes her eyes.) Go on living. We must live.

IRINA: (putting her arm around OLGA) A time will come when everyone will know what all this was for. These sufferings, these mysteries—it will all be clear. But in the meantime we must live—we must work, only work. Tomorrow I shall go alone. I shall teach in the school, and give my whole life to those who may need it. It's autumn now. Winter will come and cover everything in snow—and I shall work, I shall work.

OLGA: (embracing both sisters) The music is so gay, so brave, and one wants to live! Oh, God! Time will pass and we shall be forgotten. But our suffering will pass into joy for those who live after us, happiness and peace will dwell on earth, and there will be kind words and gentle eyes for those who are living now—in memory of us. O darling sisters, our life is not finished yet. We shall live! The music is so gay, so joyful, and it seems that in a little while we shall know why we live, why we suffer. If we only knew, if we only knew!
(The music grows fainter and fainter. The curtain falls slowly.)
CURTAIN.
